skill.name: ai-handler
skill.description: Guide Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through Replicate AI generation workflows with Inngest jobs, S3 persistence, and credit tracking.
skill.summary: Build queued Replicate AI generation flows with storage and credit handling.
skill.author: AayushBaniya2006

security_audit.summary: Static external command and script findings are false positives caused by Markdown backticks and TypeScript documentation examples. The skill is publishable with warnings because its example workflow sends user prompts to Replicate, uses an environment API token, and fetches a model output URL server-side without validation.

medium_findings.title: Third-Party Prompt Disclosure
medium_findings.description: The example forwards body.prompt through the job event and sends it to Replicate as model input. This is intended functionality, but deployments must treat user prompts as data shared with a third-party AI provider.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code example directly passes the user prompt into a Replicate prediction request. This is a real privacy consideration, although it matches the skill purpose.

medium_findings.title: Unvalidated Server-Side Fetch of Model Output URL
medium_findings.description: The example downloads finalPrediction.output[0] with fetch before uploading the bytes to S3. If the output URL is attacker-controlled or malformed, an implementation could fetch unexpected internal or external resources.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The server-side fetch is explicit and has no URL validation in the example. Replicate output is normally trusted, so legitimate use is possible.

low_findings.title: Legitimate Environment Secret Usage Requires Care
low_findings.description: The skill requires REPLICATE_API_TOKEN and uses process.env.REPLICATE_API_TOKEN to configure the Replicate client. This is appropriate, but users must avoid logging or exposing the token in generated code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment token reference is clear and normal for API access. No evidence shows token exfiltration or unsafe logging.

low_findings.title: Static Backtick Command Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick execution locations are Markdown inline code and fenced TypeScript examples. No executable shell command invocation is present in the reviewed skill files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain Markdown formatting, file paths, or TypeScript template snippets. I found no Ruby code or shell execution semantics in those locations.

low_findings.title: Static Dynamic Function Finding Is a False Positive
low_findings.description: The reported dynamic function constructor at SKILL.md line 75 is the heading for an Inngest function section. It is documentation text, not a JavaScript Function constructor or eval pattern.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 75 is a Markdown heading. There is no dynamic code construction at that location.

low_findings.title: Static Weak Crypto and Reconnaissance Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported weak cryptography and system reconnaissance locations do not contain cryptographic algorithms or host reconnaissance commands. They are frontmatter, prose, or user and record identifiers in examples.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited text shows metadata, architecture prose, credit deduction, and job payload identifiers. No evidence of weak cryptography or system reconnaissance was found.

dangerous_patterns.title: Server-Side External URL Download
dangerous_patterns.description: The example performs fetch(outputUrl) on a model output URL before storing the result. Production implementations should restrict protocols, hosts, content types, and response size.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code pattern is visible and lacks validation in the snippet. The usual Replicate flow reduces but does not remove the concern.

dangerous_patterns.title: External AI Provider Request With User Data
dangerous_patterns.description: The example sends a user prompt to Replicate. Applications should disclose provider processing and apply prompt retention, safety, and privacy controls.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The prompt is included in Replicate prediction input. The behavior is intended but has privacy impact.

content.value_statement: AI generation workflows can lose long jobs, files, and credit state. This skill guides a queued Replicate workflow with Inngest, S3 storage, and database status updates.
actual_capabilities: Defines an API route pattern for authenticated AI generation requests.
Shows credit checks and credit deduction before queueing work.
Creates database records with processing, completed, and failed states.
Queues Replicate prediction work through an Inngest background function.
Downloads completed model output and persists it through an S3 upload helper.
Updates database state after success or failure.
limitations: Provides documentation and example code, not a complete installed package.
Assumes existing Replicate, Inngest, S3, database, and credit helpers.
Does not include webhook verification or output URL validation.
Does not implement automatic credit refunds after failed generations.

use_cases.title: Add AI Image Generation
use_cases.description: Create an authenticated route that queues Replicate image work and returns a processing status quickly.

use_cases.title: Protect Paid Generation Credits
use_cases.description: Connect credit checks, job creation, and failure handling around expensive model calls.

use_cases.title: Persist Temporary AI Outputs
use_cases.description: Move model output from temporary provider URLs into durable S3 storage tied to database records.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use ai-handler to outline the API route, job queue, Replicate call, S3 upload, and database status changes for image generation.

prompt_templates.title: Adapt to My Schema
prompt_templates.prompt: Use ai-handler with my generations table and auth helper. Map each field needed for status, prompt, user ID, and output URL.

prompt_templates.title: Add Failure Handling
prompt_templates.prompt: Use ai-handler to design retry, failed status, and optional credit refund behavior for Replicate prediction failures.

prompt_templates.title: Harden the Output Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Use ai-handler to review my Replicate output download flow for URL validation, content limits, S3 metadata, and privacy risks.

output_examples.input: Request a Replicate generation workflow for a Next.js app.
output: Authenticated route accepts a prompt and checks credits.
A processing record is created before the background event is sent.
The worker calls Replicate, waits for completion, uploads the result, and updates status.

output_examples.input: Ask how failed predictions should affect credit state.
output_examples.output: The response recommends marking the record failed, surfacing the error through the job system, and adding a clear refund policy.

output_examples.input: Ask how to store Replicate output safely.
output_examples.output: The response explains that temporary provider URLs should be downloaded server-side, validated, size-limited, and copied into durable object storage.

best_practices: Validate prompts, output URLs, response size, and content type before storage.
Keep provider tokens in environment secrets and never return them to clients.
Record every status transition so users can see pending, completed, and failed work.
anti_patterns: Do not run long Replicate predictions inside a synchronous request handler.
Do not trust provider output URLs without validation and size limits.
Do not deduct credits without a documented failure and refund policy.

faq.question: Does this skill include runnable source code?
faq.answer: It provides implementation guidance and TypeScript examples. You must adapt the examples to your project helpers and schema.

faq.question: Which AI provider does it target?
faq.answer: It targets Replicate models through the Replicate JavaScript client.

faq.question: Why does it use Inngest?
faq.answer: Inngest moves long model predictions into a background worker with retries and status tracking.

faq.question: Can it work without S3?
faq.answer: Yes, but you need another durable storage service for temporary model outputs.

faq.question: Does it handle user credits?
faq.answer: It shows where to check and deduct credits. Refund behavior is left as a product decision.

faq.question: What security review is needed before production?
faq.answer: Review prompt privacy, provider terms, secret handling, output URL validation, content limits, and webhook verification.
